perturbation.original_text: (b) From and after the date on which a Receivable or Participation Interest, as applicable, is sold to the Owner, the Servicer shall service and administer each related Serviced Asset for the benefit of the Owner and shall extend, amend or otherwise modify such Serviced Asset, by complying in all material respects with the following (collectively, the "Servicing Standard"): (A) reasonable care, using that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises with respect to comparable receivables that it services for itself or others, and (B) Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) From and after the date on which a Receivable or Participation Interest, as applicable, is sold to the Owner, the Servicer shall endeavor to service and administer each related Serviced Asset for the benefit of the Owner and may extend, amend or otherwise modify such Serviced Asset, by generally complying with the following (collectively, the "Servicing Standard"): (A) reasonable care, using that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises with respect to comparable receivables that it services for itself or others, and (B) Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the servicer to 'shall service and administer' which is a clear obligation. This is changed to 'shall endeavor to service and administer' and 'may extend, amend, or modify,' weakening the obligation. The phrase 'by complying in all material respects' is changed to 'by generally complying' This introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less strict.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York GOL § 5-701, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain agreements to be in writing and contain clear terms. By making the servicing obligation ambiguous ('shall endeavor' and 'may extend'), the agreement becomes less clear on essential terms, potentially rendering it unenforceable under NY law because the specific obligations of the servicer are not definitively stated.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the servicer's obligations, changing 'shall service and administer' to 'shall endeavor to service and administer' and 'may extend, amend, or modify,' making the obligation less strict. The phrase 'by complying in all material respects' is also changed to 'by generally complying,' which introduces ambiguity. The Law Explanation suggests this ambiguity could render the agreement unenforceable under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY GOL § 5-701, states that agreements must be in writing, implying the terms must be clear and definite. The original contract used clear and direct language. The changed contract introduces ambiguity. Therefore the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: (c) To the fullest extent permitted by law, the Owner shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Servicer and its successors, assigns, directors, officers, agents, employees and servants (collectively, the "Servicer Indemnified Parties") from and against, any and all liabilities, obligations, losses, damages, taxes, claims, actions and suits, and any and all reasonable out-of-pocket costs, expenses and disbursements (including reasonable legal fees and expenses) of any kind and nature whatsoever (collectively, "Liabilities") which may at any time be imposed on, incurred by, or asserted against the Servicer or any Servicer Indemnified Party in any way relating to or arising out of this Agreement or any other Loan Document, the Serviced Assets or any action or inaction of the Owner or any other Person; provided, that the Owner shall not be liable for or required to indemnify a Servicer Indemnified Party from and against expenses arising or resulting from such Servicer Indemnified Party's own willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The Owner will consider indemnifying, defending and holding harmless the Servicer and its successors, assigns, directors, officers, agents, employees and servants (collectively, the "Servicer Indemnified Parties") from and against, any and all liabilities, obligations, losses, damages, taxes, claims, actions and suits, and any and all out-of-pocket costs, expenses and disbursements (including legal fees and expenses) of any kind and nature whatsoever (collectively, "Liabilities") which may at any time be imposed on, incurred by, or asserted against the Servicer or any Servicer Indemnified Party relating to or arising out of this Agreement or any other Loan Document, the Serviced Assets or any action or inaction of the Owner or any other Person; provided, that the Owner shall not indemnify a Servicer Indemnified Party from and against expenses arising or resulting from such Servicer Indemnified Party's own actions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless' to 'will consider indemnifying' introduces ambiguity. It makes the indemnification discretionary rather than mandatory. Removing 'reasonable' before 'out-of-pocket costs' and 'legal fees' also introduces ambiguity, potentially opening the door to disputes over what costs are covered. Changing 'willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence' to 'own actions' is overly broad and vague.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 prohibits agreements that exempt owners and contractors from liability for their own negligence. By making the indemnification discretionary ('will consider'), it creates uncertainty about whether the Servicer will be protected from its own negligence. Furthermore, the original clause excluded indemnification for 'willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence'. This has been changed to exclude expenses arising from 'own actions' which is overly broad, potentially including simple negligence. This may violate the spirit of NY GOL § 5-322.1, which seeks to prevent parties from contracting away liability for their own fault, even if it falls short of gross negligence, especially because the 'will consider' language leaves this protection at the discretion of the indemnifier.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that could undermine the legal obligations regarding negligence. The original text mandates indemnification 'to the fullest extent permitted by law,' while the changed text uses 'will consider indemnifying,' making it discretionary. NY GOL § 5-322.1 states that agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. By making indemnification discretionary, the modified text creates uncertainty about protecting the Servicer from its own negligence, potentially violating the intent of the law to prevent parties from contracting away liability for their own fault. The original text excluded indemnification for 'willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence,' whereas the modified text excludes 'own actions,' which is overly broad and could encompass simple negligence, further undermining the protection intended by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: (f) The Servicer shall maintain appropriate books of account and records relating to services performed hereunder, which books of account and records shall be accessible for inspection by the Owner at any time during normal business hours.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) The Servicer will endeavor to maintain books of account and records relating to services performed hereunder, which books of account and records should be accessible for review by the Owner during business hours.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly mandates that 'the Servicer shall maintain appropriate books of account and records'. Changing 'shall maintain' to 'will endeavor to maintain' makes the obligation to keep accurate records less stringent and reliable. Also, 'accessible for inspection' is changed to 'should be accessible for review' which removes the certainty of access and introduces an element of discretion. Removing 'at any time during normal' from 'normal business hours' makes the timeframe for review less clear.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY BCL § 624 grants shareholders the right to inspect corporate books and records for a proper purpose. While the Owner isn't explicitly a shareholder in the Servicer, this section of the contract is specifying access rights similar to that right. By changing 'shall maintain' to 'will endeavor to maintain' and changing 'accessible for inspection' to 'should be accessible for review', the agreement creates uncertainty regarding the reliability of the records and the Owner's ability to properly audit the Servicer, essentially weakening a right analogous to the inspection right granted under NY BCL § 624. The removal of the phrase 'at any time during normal' reduces the ability to have reasonable audit windows.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-11-28 2014-12-05 2015-01-09 2015-04-24 2015-11-27 2016-01-22 2016-03-11 2016-08-26 2016-11-25 2017-04-28 2017-05-05 2017-09-15 2018-01-05 2018-04-27 2018-10-05 2019-10-25 2019-11-29 2020-01-31 2020-02-07 2020-06-19 2020-06-26 2020-07-03 2021-04-09 2021-04-23 2021-11-12 2021-12-03 2022-02-04 2022-07-29 2023-01-06 2023-12-15 2024-03-08 2024-06-14 2024-07-26 Share Facebook Email SECTION 624 Books and records; right of inspection, prima facie evidence Business Corporation (BSC) CHAPTER 4, ARTICLE 6 § 624. Books and records; right of inspection, prima facie evidence. (a) Each corporation shall keep correct and complete books and records of account and shall keep minutes of the proceedings of its shareholders, board and executive committee, if any, and shall keep at the office of the corporation in this state or at the office of its transfer agent or registrar in this state, a record containing the names and addresses of all shareholders, the number and class of shares held by each and the dates when they respectively became the owners of record thereof. Any of the foregoing books, minutes or records may be in written form or in any other form capable of being converted into written form within a reasonable time. (b) Any person who shall have been a shareholder of record of a corporation upon at least five days' written demand shall have the right to examine in person or by agent or attorney, during usual business hours, its minutes of the proceedings of its shareholders and record of shareholders and to make extracts therefrom for any purpose reasonably related to such person's interest as a shareholder. Holders of voting trust certificates representing shares of the corporation shall be regarded as shareholders for the purpose of this section. Any such agent or attorney shall be authorized in a writing that satisfies the requirements of a writing under paragraph (b) of section 609 (Proxies). A corporation requested to provide information pursuant to this paragraph shall make available such information in written form and in any other format in which such information is maintained by the corporation and shall not be required to provide such information in any other format. If a request made pursuant to this paragraph includes a request to furnish information regarding beneficial owners, the corporation shall make available such information in its posses
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain and provide access to books and records, creating a contradiction. The original text uses 'shall maintain' and 'accessible for inspection', indicating a firm requirement. The changed text uses 'will endeavor to maintain' and 'should be accessible for review', which introduces ambiguity. NY BCL § 624(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states 'Each corporation shall keep correct and complete books and records of account'. The modified contract language weakens this requirement, allowing the Servicer to potentially avoid maintaining complete records. Additionally, the phrase 'at any time during normal' is removed, reducing the ability to have reasonable audit windows, as described in the Law Explanation. The modification undermines the certainty of access to records, contradicting the intent of maintaining accurate records for review.
